name: Whitepaper

json_data.html:
Field Guide · The Analytics Stack
Whitepaper · 2026 Edition

The AI-Ready Analytics Stack

Semantic layers, MCP, and embedded delivery in the age of AI agents — a practical, vendor-neutral field guide to what's actually working now, and why most teams aren't ready for it yet.

PF Patrick Fox
Independent technical perspective
~14 min read No vendor allegiance
Force 01
Semantic & Headless
Govern your metrics once, in a data dictionary, and serve every surface through APIs — not a dozen tools each redefining "revenue."
Force 02
Agentic & MCP
Open the stack to AI agents through a standard protocol, so they query and build against governed definitions, not screen-scrapes.
Force 03
Embedded & White-Label
Deliver analytics inside the product, in the customer's brand — as durable, governed pages, not just throwaway artifacts.
01Executive Summary 02The Shift 03Reference Architecture 04The Semantic Layer 05AI Readiness 06MCP, APIs & Agentic Loops 07Embedded & White-Label 08Artifacts vs. Pages 09Governance & Security 10A Strategy You Can Run
Executive Summary

01 —The stack is being recomposed around AI

Three forces are reshaping analytics at once — and a fourth, unglamorous one decides whether any of them actually pay off: whether your data is ready.

The first force is the semantic layer: pulling metric definitions out of individual dashboards into one governed, API-first model that every consumer shares. The second is delivery — analytics embedded inside products and customer portals, white-labeled, multi-tenant, where the work actually happens. The third, and newest, is autonomy: AI agents that don't just answer questions but query governed data, assemble views, and operate the stack through an open protocol — the Model Context Protocol (MCP).

These reinforce each other. A semantic layer makes embedding clean and gives agents something trustworthy to call. MCP turns "API-first" into "agent-operable." Embedded delivery is where governed insight reaches a human. Put together, they describe a composable analytics stack: governed once, consumed everywhere — by people and by machines.

But there's a catch the demos skip. Most data isn't AI-ready. It's undocumented, inconsistently defined, and ungoverned — and an agent pointed at messy data doesn't fail loudly, it produces confident, wrong answers at machine speed. This guide walks the whole stack — semantic layer, MCP, embedding, governance — and is honest about the readiness work that has to come first.

The thesis in one line

The winning analytics platform isn't the one with the prettiest dashboard. It's the one that's governed once, embeddable anywhere, and safely operable by an agent — sitting on data that's actually ready for it.

  • Define once, consume everywhere — a governed semantic layer + data dictionary.
  • Open the stack to agents — through MCP, not brittle integrations.
  • Embed where work happens — white-label, multi-tenant, in-product.
  • Earn readiness first — messy data is the real blocker, not the model.
Market Context

02 —From dashboards to data products to agents

For two decades, BI meant a tool you logged into and a dashboard most people never opened. That model is being unbundled on three fronts — each already a real product category with serious vendors behind it.

The semantic layer

Cube, the dbt Semantic Layer, GoodData, Looker (LookML) and AtScale expose governed metric definitions over APIs such as SQL, REST and GraphQL — "define once, consume everywhere." The presentation layer becomes a choice, not a cage.

Govern

Embedded analytics

Product and data teams are putting analytics inside their own apps and customer portals — white-labeled, multi-tenant, SSO-secured. Purpose-built platforms (Luzmo, Embeddable, GoodData) and the embedded SKUs of Tableau, Power BI and ThoughtSpot all chase this surface.

Deliver

Agentic analytics

Natural-language is giving way to agents that build and act: ThoughtSpot's Spotter, Tableau's agentic analytics, Google Looker's conversational agents, Databricks' Genie. The frontier is authoring, not just answering.

Automate

The center of gravity is moving from "which dashboard do I open?" to "what do I want — and which surface, or agent, delivers it?"

Reference Architecture

03 —One governed core, many consumers

The modern stack is a pipeline with a governed waist. Data is moved and modeled once, defined once in a semantic layer, and then consumed by many surfaces — humans and machines — through the same governed contract.

The Foundation

04 —The semantic layer & the data dictionary

A semantic layer is the governed model of your business — what "active customer," "revenue," or "on-time" actually mean, defined in one place and exposed through APIs. It is the single highest-leverage investment in an AI-ready stack.

Define once, consume everywhere

Without it, the same metric is re-implemented — slightly differently — in every dashboard, notebook and export, and no two numbers agree. A semantic layer makes the definition the source of truth, so a BI tool, an embedded app, a reverse-ETL job and an AI agent all compute the same figure. Cube, the dbt Semantic Layer (MetricFlow), GoodData, Looker's LookML and AtScale are different bets on this same idea.

The data dictionary is the contract

The semantic layer's documentation — the data dictionary — is what makes data legible to both humans and machines: every metric and field with a plain-language definition, owner, grain, lineage and allowed values. For people it ends the "what does this column mean?" tax. For agents it's the difference between reasoning over a named, described metric and guessing at a raw column called amt_2.

From a destination to a service

The quiet, decisive shift: analytics stops being a place you go and becomes a service other things call. That's exactly what makes embedding clean, pipelines composable, and — critically — the whole stack legible to an AI agent.

The Reality Check

05 —Most data isn't AI-ready — and that's the real blocker

The model is rarely the bottleneck. The data is. Pointed at undocumented, inconsistent, ungoverned data, an agent doesn't error out — it produces confident, plausible, wrong answers, faster than anyone can check them.

The uncomfortable truth

"AI-ready data" is not a model problem you can prompt your way out of. It's a data-quality, definition, and governance problem — and for most organizations it's mostly unsolved. Garbage in, agentic garbage out, at scale.

Readiness is concrete. Data is ready for AI when it clears five bars — and an honest audit usually finds a team is strong on one or two and weak on the rest:

1 · Modeled

Metrics live in a semantic layer, not re-derived per query. An agent should call net_revenue, not reverse-engineer it from raw tables and get it subtly wrong.

2 · Documented

A real data dictionary: every field and metric has a definition, owner, grain and allowed values. Undocumented data is a liability the moment a machine starts reading it autonomously.

3 · Trustworthy

Tested and fresh: quality checks, null/range rules, and known freshness, so consumers can tell whether a number is current and correct — or quietly stale.

4 · Traceable

Lineage from source to metric, so an answer can be explained and audited — non-negotiable once an agent, not a person, is producing the number.

5 · Discoverable

Catalogued and permissioned, so the right data is findable and the wrong data is unreachable. An agent should never be able to surface what a person couldn't.

The honest move

Audit against these five before you ship an agent. Readiness isn't a gate to clear once — it's the work that makes everything downstream trustworthy. The semantic layer and data dictionary are where most of it lands.

The Headline

06 —MCP, APIs & the agentic loop

An agent is only as useful as what it can safely reach. The Model Context Protocol is how a governed stack becomes something an AI agent can operate — by calling real, permissioned capabilities, not scraping a screen.

What MCP is

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is a standard open-sourced by Anthropic in November 2024 for connecting AI applications to external tools and data through one universal interface. Before MCP, every AI-to-system connection was a bespoke integration — an N×M tangle of custom glue. MCP replaces it with one protocol of hosts (the AI app), clients, and servers (which expose tools and data), and has since been adopted across the industry, including by OpenAI and Google in 2025.

Why analytics needs it

A semantic layer already exposes governed capabilities over APIs. MCP gives an agent a standard, discoverable way to use them: list the metrics, run a governed query, read the data dictionary, assemble a view — each a typed tool call with real arguments, inside the existing permission model. Semantic-layer vendors are already shipping MCP endpoints; the agent isn't guessing at a UI, it's operating the system through its own front door.

The loop is only as good as the data

An agentic loop — plan, call a tool, read the result, decide the next call — compounds quality and error. Pointed at a governed semantic layer with a clean data dictionary, it's remarkable. Pointed at raw, undocumented tables, it confidently compounds mistakes. Good data is the fuel; MCP is just the fuel line.

The platforms that win the agent era won't be the ones that bolt a chatbot onto a dashboard. They'll be the ones an agent can safely operate from the inside.

Delivery

07 —Embedded analytics & white-labeling

Insight has to live where the work happens — inside a product, a customer portal, a partner extranet — wearing the host's brand, not a vendor's. Embedding is now the default delivery surface for customer-facing data, and it has its own discipline.

Embed, don't redirect

Analytics inside your application beats a separate tool users must go visit. The strongest experiences feel native: same navigation, same brand, same login — not an iframe that screams "third-party."

In-product

White-label, end to end

Your logo, colors, fonts and domain — theming that restyles every surface from a central palette so embedded analytics reads as a feature of your product, not a guest inside it.

Brand

Multi-tenant by design

SSO plus row- and role-level security so each customer — and each user — sees only their own slice. Tenant isolation is the hard part of embedding; get it wrong once and it's a breach, not a bug.

Isolation

Interactive, not static

Filters, drill-downs and write-back turn embedded reports into data apps. The bar has risen: customers expect to act on the data in place, not export it to a spreadsheet.

Experience

Pick your embedding model

iframe with secure tokens, JavaScript/SDK embeds, or API-first headless components you render yourself. More control means more to maintain — choose deliberately, per how custom the surface must feel.

Strategy

The market is crowded

Purpose-built embedding platforms (Luzmo, Embeddable, GoodData, Sisense) compete with the embedded SKUs of Tableau, Power BI and ThoughtSpot. Evaluate on theming depth, tenant security, and how cleanly they expose a semantic layer — not chart count.

Landscape
An Embedding Strategy Decision

08 —One-time artifacts vs. persistent pages

AI makes it nearly free to generate a chart, an answer, or a whole report on demand. That's powerful — and it quietly creates a new strategic fork that most teams haven't named yet.

One-time artifacts

An agent-generated, throwaway output: "show me churn by plan this quarter," rendered once and discarded. Ephemeral, personal, instant. Perfect for exploration, ad-hoc questions, and a draft you'll refine in conversation.

  • Strength: zero setup, fits the exact question, disposable.
  • Risk: no governance, no review, no shared source of truth — dangerous as a number people act on.

Persistent pages

A durable, governed, versioned surface — a dashboard or embedded data product that's owned, maintained and trusted. Shared, reviewed, accountable. The right home for a metric the business runs on.

  • Strength: governance, lineage, version control, a single source of truth.
  • Cost: someone owns it — it has to be built, reviewed and maintained.
The best practice

Use both, deliberately: let agents spin up artifacts for exploration and one-off answers, then promote the valuable ones to persistent pages — governed, owned, embedded. The lifecycle is prompt → artifact → promote. Trouble starts when a throwaway artifact quietly becomes the number the business runs on, with no owner and no review behind it.

Governance & Security

09 —The part that makes all of it shippable

Embedding multiplies your blast radius. Agents multiply your action space. Both make governance the precondition for everything else — not an afterthought you add at the end.

Identity & access

SSO over standard protocols (SAML, OAuth/OIDC) with role-based access and tenant isolation, so the right person — and the right customer — sees exactly the right slice, and nothing more. This is the same gate humans and agents both pass through.

Data protection & lineage

Encryption in transit and at rest, plus lineage from source to metric so any answer can be explained and audited. When a machine is producing the number, "where did this come from?" has to have an instant, provable answer.

Govern the agent, not just the user

An agent should inherit the same permission model as a person, never a privileged side door. Scope its tools, filter by tenant, rate-limit it, and keep a human in the loop for anything that writes — security built into the loop, not bolted on after.

Validation & version control

Treat analytics like production code: definitions, pages and agent-authored changes validated for structure and dangling references, tracked in version control, reviewable and reversible. Auditability is what lets you trust autonomy.

Data & Embedding Strategy

10 —A strategy you can actually run

You can build the whole stack yourself, buy a bundled BI suite, or compose best-of-breed around a semantic layer. The right answer depends on where your differentiated value really lives — a qualitative comparison, free of invented numbers:

Dimension Build it all yourself Bundled BI suite Composable / semantic-first
Metric governance You design and police consistency by hand Governed, but inside one vendor's walls A semantic layer every tool and agent shares
Embedding & white-label Full control, full maintenance burden Strong if you live in that vendor's stack Best-of-breed embedding on open definitions
AI / agent readiness A research project on top of a big project Improving, but tied to the vendor's roadmap MCP-friendly: agents call a governed layer
Lock-in None — and none of the leverage either High — definitions live in the tool Low — swap surfaces, keep the semantic core
Time to value Quarters — everything from scratch Fast inside the suite, slow at its edges Fast once the semantic layer exists
Where effort goes Rebuilding undifferentiated infrastructure Adapting to the suite's way of working The metrics and experience that are yours

Directional comparison for decision-making, not a benchmark. Most teams land on a hybrid — a bundled suite for internal BI, a composable semantic-first approach for the customer-facing, AI-touched, embedded surfaces that differentiate them.

Start here

Stand up a semantic layer and data dictionary before you chase agents. It's the foundation embedding, governance and AI-readiness all depend on.

Step 1

Earn readiness

Audit your data against the five bars in §05. Document, test and catalogue the metrics that matter — before an agent ever touches them.

Step 2

Open it to agents

Expose the governed layer over MCP and APIs, behind the governance gate. Let agents explore with artifacts; promote the keepers to persistent pages.

Step 3

Govern once. Embed anywhere. Let agents operate — safely.

That's the shape of the modern analytics stack — and it's buildable today, if the data underneath is ready. Take the architecture in this guide and pressure-test it against your own.

Explore the Model Context Protocol Back to the top
